note: DELTA scouting reports v2 — football-story discipline: play style, archetype, how the player wins, situation history. Deliberately excludes ages, market prices, buy/sell verdicts, and career-rate percentages (all drift). Fixed history (named seasons, draft pedigree) only. Live data profile + price math live in The Read; these complement, never duplicate. Regenerating in batches; players not yet covered show the graceful fallback.

reports.Ja'Marr Chase: A route-runner with sprinter finish — wins at every level of the field and turns slants into house calls like nobody else in the sport. Cincinnati's entire passing identity is built on getting him the ball early and letting the run-after-catch do the violence.
reports.Jahmyr Gibbs: A satellite back with a bell-cow's between-the-tackles courage — home-run speed that Detroit weaponizes in space and on angle routes. The rare modern back whose receiving chops and rushing burst are both plus tools rather than one masking the other.
reports.Josh Allen: The most physically overwhelming quarterback of his era — a howitzer arm attached to a tight end's frame that converts short-yardage like a fullback. The rushing dimension is scheme-proof, which is why his weekly floor has survived every version of Buffalo's supporting cast.
reports.Bijan Robinson: The complete-back prototype: vision, contact balance, receiving polish, and the patience to let blocks develop that most backs never learn. His 2025 season was the version scouts promised at the draft — an offense finally willing to run through him.
reports.Puka Nacua: A physical possession receiver who plays much faster than his timed speed — elite at the catch point, ruthless after it, and schemed touches near the line because he breaks first tackles like a back. McVay's offense treats him as the answer to every third down.
reports.Jaxon Smith-Njigba: A slot technician with rare short-area suddenness — creates separation in phone-booth spaces where most receivers need runway. Seattle rebuilt its passing tree around his option routes, and the 2025 breakout was the offense catching up to the skill.
reports.Amon-Ra St. Brown: The definition of earned volume — runs the exact depth the chains require, catches everything in traffic, and blocks like he's trying to make the practice squad. Detroit's offense runs through his reliability rather than his explosiveness, by design.
reports.Drake Maye: A big-armed rhythm passer with sneaky escape ability — the arm talent was never in question, and year two showed the processing catching up. New England has its first genuine franchise-QB trajectory since Brady left.
reports.Justin Jefferson: Still the most complete route technician of his generation — release packages, tempo control, and ball skills that don't age the way speed does. The 2025 collapse tracked Minnesota's quarterback chaos game for game; nothing on his tape suggests the player broke.
reports.Trey McBride: A tight end who plays like a possession receiver with a lineman's temperament — Arizona's first read on every passing down and the most target-secure player at the position. Wins with body positioning and hands rather than seam speed, which is why it repeats weekly.
reports.Brock Bowers: A generational receiving talent at the position — moves like a big slot, catches like a WR1, and was productive from his first NFL snap. The 2025 injury paused what his rookie year made obvious: the archetype every offense is hunting for.
reports.Ashton Jeanty: A contact-balance marvel — the tackle-breaking that defined his college tape translated immediately, even while the surrounding offense gave him nothing. The boom-bust rookie output was a line-and-scheme problem wearing his jersey number.
reports.Joe Burrow: Surgical from the pocket with the best anticipatory accuracy in the league — when protected, he processes coverage faster than defenses can disguise it. The injury history is now part of the profile; the per-snap brilliance has never wavered.
reports.CeeDee Lamb: A YAC-first alpha who wins with route savvy and open-field creativity rather than pure burst. Dallas's passing volume collapse took his counting stats hostage — the skill set that produced 2023 hasn't gone anywhere.
reports.Caleb Williams: An improvisational creator with elite arm elasticity — throws from platforms most quarterbacks can't even set from. Year two showed real structural growth within the offense; the off-script magic was always there.
reports.Lamar Jackson: The most dangerous open-field runner ever to play the position, paired with a passing game that quietly became elite in his late-20s prime. Every projection is a referendum on how rushing quarterbacks age — his tape keeps refusing to answer.
reports.Patrick Mahomes: Still the standard for off-platform arm talent and late-down wizardry — the 2025 rebound showed the ceiling remains when Kansas City gives him functional weapons. The dynasty-era weekly dominance has become elite-but-mortal production.
reports.De'Von Achane: Warp-speed in pads — the fastest ball-carrier in the league with receiving skills that force linebacker mismatches every snap he's on the field. Miami has always rationed his touches; the per-touch output has never justified the caution.
reports.Jayden Daniels: A surgical dual-threat — processes quickly, throws with touch to every level, and takes off with a sprinter's acceleration when the pocket breaks. His rookie season was one of the great QB debuts ever; the 2025 injury paused it mid-sentence.
reports.Jonathan Taylor: A downhill power-speed back who reclaimed a full workhorse role and looked like the 2021 version doing it — decisive cuts, finishing runs, and fresh legs from the lost seasons in between. Indianapolis rebuilt its identity around feeding him.
reports.Omarion Hampton: A violent, north-south runner with soft hands — the first-round pedigree showed immediately in his pad level and pass-protection maturity. The rookie sample was short; the workload trust was instant.
reports.Drake London: A contested-catch monster with a power forward's frame — boxes out defenders and wins jump balls that shouldn't be catchable. The availability record is the whole conversation, because the per-game target gravity has been alpha-level from day one.
reports.Justin Herbert: Arm talent that has no ceiling and never has — velocity, touch, and off-platform accuracy in one frame. The career-long puzzle is an offense that converts it into spike weeks; the tools have outrun every scheme he's played in.
reports.James Cook: A slasher with elite vision in outside-zone — turned Buffalo's backfield from a committee into a possession, one efficient season at a time. Not a power back and doesn't pretend to be; wins by never taking negative plays.
reports.Trevor Lawrence: The pedigree finally met the production in 2025 — cleaner mechanics, faster decisions, and an offense that stopped fighting him. Still more accumulator than slate-breaker, but the franchise-QB floor is finally real.
reports.George Pickens: A vertical terror with the best sideline ball skills in the league — catches things that defy body geometry. Dallas gave him the alpha usage Pittsburgh never would, and the boom weeks arrived on schedule; the quiet ones still come with them.
reports.Tetairoa McMillan: A long-striding possession-X with elite catch radius — Carolina made him the immediate focal point and he handled alpha coverage as a rookie. The polish will decide whether the volume converts; the frame and hands are already NFL-proven.
reports.Malik Nabers: Target gravity you can't teach — defenders know the ball is coming and he wins anyway, with run-after-catch that turns hitches into chunk plays. The injury interrupted a trajectory that looked like a top-five-receiver arc.
reports.Colston Loveland: A move tight end with receiver fluidity — Chicago used him like a big slot immediately, and the rookie flashes came against real coverage. The archetype (and the draft capital) say featured role; the position says be patient.
reports.Brock Purdy: The system-QB debate died of exhaustion — precision, timing, and pocket movement that keep producing regardless of who's healthy around him. Never the arm-talent argument, always the results one.
reports.Bo Nix: A processing-speed quarterback who gets the ball out on time and takes what's schemed — Denver's offense runs on his decisiveness rather than his arm. The ceiling question is fair; the floor has been bankable from week one.
reports.Jalen Hurts: The most efficient short-yardage weapon in football history and a steadily improved deep-ball thrower — the rushing TD floor is a cheat code no other QB profile offers. The new coordinator inherits an offense built on his legs' availability.
reports.Jeremiyah Love: An explosive one-cut back with elite long speed and natural receiving hands — the cleanest RB prospect since Bijan by most boards. Arizona spent the #3 pick on the idea that he's a franchise offense's centerpiece, not a committee piece.
reports.Tyler Warren: A do-everything tight end — lined up inline, in the slot, in the backfield, and at wildcat QB as a rookie. Indianapolis found ways to feed him that the position's usual learning curve doesn't allow, which says everything about the skill set.
reports.Nico Collins: A crafty big-bodied X who wins with body control and late hands — quarterback-friendly in every sense. The spike weeks have never matched the target quality; the week-to-week trust has.
reports.Jaxson Dart: A rhythm passer with functional wheels who arrived more NFL-ready than the draft slot suggested — quick game, play-action touch, and poise in muddy pockets as a rookie. The Giants may have stumbled into the answer.
reports.Chris Olave: One of the smoothest route-runners in football — separation at will when healthy, with the concussion file being the only reason the league doesn't talk about him that way. The 2025 rebound was the talent reasserting itself.
reports.Rashee Rice: A YAC machine built for Kansas City's spacing — turns flat routes into first downs and shallow crossers into touchdowns. Every stretch of availability has produced at an elite per-game clip; the stretches keep getting interrupted.
reports.Jordan Love: A live-armed rhythm passer whose 2023 finish remains the tantalizing tape — layered throws, aggression, and touch. The two seasons since have been competent without recapturing it; the tools say it's still in there.
reports.Chase Brown: A one-speed volume back — that speed being genuinely fast — who earned Cincinnati's trust with pass protection and ball security. The role is the asset; the explosiveness shows in flashes rather than averages.
reports.Tucker Kraft: A power-catch tight end with genuine run-after-contact — plays the position like a fullback who learned routes, which is a compliment. The 2025 injury cut short what was becoming a legitimate featured-role breakout.
reports.Dak Prescott: A rhythm-and-anticipation passer who has quietly aged like a professional — the 2025 rebound came on timing and command, not athleticism. The profile is all present-tense value; nobody is buying the future.
reports.Kenneth Walker III: A big-play hitter with elite lateral cut — the most boom-dependent runner of his cohort, capable of house calls and 12-carry-30-yard days in equal measure. Kansas City is betting scheme and spacing unlock the version Seattle suppressed.
reports.TreVeyon Henderson: A speed-and-pass-pro specialist — the explosive tape is real and the third-down trust came immediately, which is rare for rookies. What's missing is early-down volume, and that's a coaching decision, not a talent verdict.
reports.Kyren Williams: A vision-and-footwork grinder who milks every blocked yard — no long speed, elite decision-making. The Rams keep paying him in touches because the offense functions when he's on schedule; the ceiling was never the pitch.
reports.Luther Burden: A YAC-first slot with running-back contact balance — the college tape showed a schemed-touches star waiting for a creative offense. Chicago's crowded room buried the rookie year; the skill set didn't change.
reports.A.J. Brown: A power receiver who bullies corners off the line and turns slants into runway — the physicality translates to every offense. New England imported the archetype specifically to give its young QB a possession-alpha who wins without perfect throws.
reports.Christian McCaffrey: The most complete offensive weapon of his generation — route-runner, pass-protector, between-the-tackles finisher — and the 2025 season proved the engine still runs at full load. The profile is a masterpiece with a countdown clock.
reports.Jaylen Waddle: Pure field-tilting speed with underrated route craft — the Miami years cast him as a complementary piece and the production followed the role downward. Denver's bet is that featured usage reveals the alpha the speed always implied.
reports.Zay Flowers: A twitchy separator with juice after the catch — wins early in routes and makes the first man miss reliably. Baltimore's run-first ecosystem caps the target quality; the skills have always graded ahead of the box scores.
reports.Breece Hall: A long-speed back with receiving-down polish — the burst that made him a top-five dynasty asset still shows in flashes between the scheme wreckage. Three offenses in, the tape argument is that the situation keeps failing the player.
reports.Jared Goff: A timing-and-touch pocket passer who executes at an elite level in structure and struggles the moment it breaks — Detroit built a scheme that never lets it break. The definition of a system's ceiling and floor in one player.
reports.Garrett Wilson: A separation artist — release, tempo, and body control that win against press on an island. The Jets have burned his prime on quarterback roulette; every evaluation is the same sentence about a different year.
reports.Quinshon Judkins: A downhill hammer with sneaky burst through the hole — Cleveland handed him early-down duty and he ran with the physicality advertised. The receiving game and pass-pro are the development items between him and every-down status.
reports.Rome Odunze: A ball-tracking specialist with prototype size — wins deep and at the boundary with late hands. Two years of crowded target trees kept the role smaller than the talent; the depth chart finally cleared.
reports.Emeka Egbuka: A polished route technician who does everything at an NFL level already — the scouting cliché 'pro-ready' made flesh. Tampa's veteran-heavy room rationed his rookie usage; the skill set needs only the queue to move.
reports.Tee Higgins: A contested-catch X with WR1 talent and WR2 usage — his career is the league's longest-running 'if he were the alpha' hypothetical. Wins above the rim and in the intermediate game whenever the targets arrive.
reports.Bucky Irving: A make-you-miss specialist with elite vision in tight quarters — wins with feet and patience rather than power or speed. Earned Tampa's trust snap by snap, which is the durable kind of role security.
reports.Sam LaPorta: A route-savvy move tight end who peaked as a rookie and has been rationed since — Detroit's offense simply stopped needing him at that volume. The skills didn't regress; the target competition arrived.
reports.Jameson Williams: Field-flipping speed that changes coverage shells by alignment alone — a vertical specialist whose weekly output rides two or three plays. The archetype is inherently volatile; his is the fastest version of it.
reports.Saquon Barkley: Still the most explosive open-field athlete the position has seen in a decade — the 2024 season was an all-timer. What follows a 400-touch masterpiece at his mileage is the oldest story in running back economics.
reports.Kyle Pitts: The athletic outlier the league has spent half a decade failing to scheme for — mismatch size and speed that finally produced a competent season in 2025. The talent-versus-usage debate is now a coaching indictment more than a player one.
reports.Cam Ward: A playground creator with a live arm and unshakable confidence — the rookie tape showed the improvisation surviving NFL speed even while the results didn't. Year-one struggles were priced into the rebuild he was drafted to lead.
A vertical field-stretcher with alpha flashes — the rookie year showed route growth well beyond the deep-threat label. The injury-wrecked sophomore season froze the evaluation right when it was getting interesting.
reports.C.J. Stroud: An anticipatory thrower with a rookie season for the ages — the two years since have been a study in what pressure and a broken supporting cast do to timing passers. The talent that produced 2023 hasn't left; the conditions have never returned.
reports.Kyler Murray: An electric dual-threat in a compact frame — when the legs are involved, the offense has answers nothing else provides. Minnesota is the bet that a real structure around him rewrites a decade of almosts.
reports.Ladd McConkey: A route-running savant — option routes, tempo, and separation that made him a rookie-year security blanket. The sophomore dip tracked the offense's collapse; the craft is the most translatable skill at the position.
reports.Tyler Shough: A veteran-aged rookie with functional arm talent and command beyond his experience — New Orleans got competence immediately. The profile is bridge-quarterback-plus unless the ceiling reveals something the tape hasn't yet.
reports.Javonte Williams: A contact-balance bruiser rebuilt from the knee — the Dallas season was the first since the injury that looked like the pre-injury player. Broken-tackle rates were always the calling card; they finally came back.
reports.DeVonta Smith: A precision route-runner with elite play strength for his frame — wins with angles and timing rather than mass. Philadelphia's run-heavy tilt starved the target tree; the craft never went anywhere.
A production machine in college who kept producing immediately — savvy route feel and after-catch strength that belie the age. Tight ends aren't supposed to be this useful this early; the profile is quietly special.
reports.Josh Jacobs: A tone-setting grinder who manufactures yards after contact and never leaves the field — Green Bay's offense leans on his dependability weekly. The style ages hard, but nothing in the current tape says it's started.
reports.Carnell Tate: A silky boundary receiver with elite ball skills and route pacing — the cleanest hands in his draft class by most evaluations. Tennessee drafted him to be the WR1, and the depth chart offers no resistance.
reports.Jordyn Tyson: A fluid separator with inside-outside versatility and top-10 pedigree — New Orleans cleared the receiver room right before drafting him. The path and the polish arrived together.
reports.Travis Etienne: A speed back who rebuilt his value in New Orleans with the burst that made him a first-rounder — one-cut acceleration and receiving chops. The between-the-tackles grind has never been the game; the space touches still electrify.
reports.Baker Mayfield: A rhythm passer playing with house money — the Tampa reinvention made him a top-12 producer on aggression and timing. The career pattern says the great years alternate with the fine ones; both are startable.
reports.Cam Skattebo: A battering ram with shockingly nimble feet — runs angry, catches everything, and made the Giants' backfield his within two months. The injury stopped a rookie year that was trending toward cult-hero volume.
reports.Fernando Mendoza: A polished pocket processor with first-round pedigree — the Raiders drafted him to end the bridge-QB carousel. Anticipation and accuracy are the sell; the athleticism is functional, not featured.
The most decorated receiver prospect in a decade — route precision, ball skills, and pedigree with two NFL seasons that stubbornly refuse to match. Nothing in the tape explains the gap, which is why nobody has stopped believing yet.
reports.Sam Darnold: A traits passer whose career is a tour of environments — brilliant in structure with Minnesota, ordinary since. The book is written: the situation is the projection.
reports.George Kittle: The most complete tight end of his era — a devastating blocker whose receiving weeks still break slates when the script allows. The all-around game is why the production has aged slower than the birthday suggests.
reports.Daniel Jones: A functional athlete and careful distributor reborn in Indianapolis — the offense asks for efficiency and his legs, and he's delivered both. The renaissance is real; the ceiling is what it always was.
reports.Alec Pierce: A vertical specialist with elite ball tracking — one of the league's best deep-ball win rates on a modest target diet. The role is narrow, deep, and quietly getting wider each season.
reports.Matthew Stafford: Still one of the best pure throwers alive — the 2025 season was arm-talent-over-age in its most literal form. Every evaluation is the same: elite until the day it isn't.
reports.Christian Watson: A size-speed freak whose flashes remain among the most electric in the league — and whose soft-tissue file remains among the thickest. The talent-availability bargain has never once resolved.
reports.Bryce Young: A processing-first point guard rebuilt from the benching — the arm limitations are real, the toughness and command turned out to be realer. The trajectory since rock bottom is one of the league's quiet respect stories.
reports.Jadarian Price: An explosive committee back with return-game juice — Seattle's new staff drafted speed on purpose. The profile is opportunity-dependent and the opportunity is genuinely open.
reports.Jake Ferguson: A tough-catch security blanket who does his best work in the red zone and on third down — Dallas's most quarterback-friendly target after the alpha. The role is stable; the ceiling is the role.
reports.D'Andre Swift: A space player who has spent a career being asked to grind between the tackles — the receiving flair and open-field wiggle remain the sell. Chicago finally used him closer to the design spec.
reports.Dalton Kincaid: A big-slot receiving talent Buffalo drafted to feature and has never actually featured — the hands and route feel show every time the targets arrive. Three years in, the usage is the entire mystery.
reports.Derrick Henry: The most physically dominant back of his generation, still stiff-arming the aging curve itself — the 2025 season was another chapter in a career that broke the actuarial tables. Every snap is historic; none of it is projectable.
reports.Ricky Pearsall: A polished separator with sticky hands — the flashes in San Francisco's crowded room have been genuinely impressive. The 49ers pecking order is the only thing between him and a real role.
reports.RJ Harvey: A compact home-run hitter with elite college production — Denver's committee rationed the rookie year to change-of-pace duty. The burst says the role should grow; the depth chart will decide.
reports.Jordan Addison: A route-running natural with alpha instincts in a WR2 body — production has tracked Minnesota's quarterback quality exactly. The craft is real; the situation writes his box scores.
reports.Makai Lemon: A twitchy YAC receiver with elite college production and top-10 capital — Philadelphia drafted him directly into the A.J. Brown vacancy. The profile is a featured slot-flanker hybrid from day one.
reports.Terry McLaurin: A decade of elite route-running wasted on quarterback chaos, finally rewarded in the Daniels era — the craft has never slipped. The evaluation now is purely about how long the athleticism holds.
reports.Mike Evans: The most reliable red-zone monster of his generation — a career built on boundary fades and body position that never needed speed to begin with. San Francisco is renting the parts of the game that don't age.
reports.David Montgomery: A between-the-tackles professional — short-area power, ball security, and TD-vulture instincts. The Houston move stripped the committee context that made the profile sing.
reports.Courtland Sutton: A boundary possession-X who wins with size and sideline footwork — Denver's most trusted target for years running. The alpha usage has always outpaced the fantasy output; that's the profile, not a phase.
reports.Xavier Worthy: Track speed that warps coverage — the league's fastest deep threat playing in an offense that refuses to be vertical. The usage-talent mismatch is total; one of them has to move.
reports.Wan'Dale Robinson: A manufactured-touch slot with running-back elusiveness — the target volume is real and earned, the depth of target permanently shallow. Tennessee bought the floor, not a ceiling.
reports.Josh Downs: A quickness-based slot separator — wins instantly off the line and works the middle fearlessly for his size. The role shrank around him in 2025; the skill set didn't.
reports.Chuba Hubbard: A patient one-cut runner who proved in 2024 he can carry a full load — vision and burst that graded out far better than the pedigree. Carolina re-crowded the room anyway; the tape says he answered the question.
reports.DK Metcalf: A physical marvel who wins with speed and power in straight lines — the route tree has always been narrow and devastating. Pittsburgh gets the archetype at the stage where the athleticism is the whole bet.
reports.Travis Hunter: The most singular athlete of his draft class attempting the most singular experiment in modern football — two-way snaps at NFL speed. The receiving talent is real; the evaluation is unprecedented by design.
reports.Davante Adams: The release-package master — still creating separation with footwork that has no dependence on burst, which is why the age curve keeps missing him. The craft is the entire profile now, and the craft is immaculate.
reports.Jaylen Warren: A low-center-of-gravity grinder with sneaky receiving value — efficiency has never been the question, just the committee math. Pittsburgh keeps importing his competition; he keeps out-producing his share.
reports.Quentin Johnston: A size-speed project that quietly stopped being a project — three straight years of growth in contested catches and route discipline. The bust narrative expired; the league hasn't fully noticed.
A left-handed deep-ball artist with effortless velocity — the arm is the argument, the caution in his early starts the counterargument. Atlanta's investment says patience; the tools say it's warranted.
reports.J.J. McCarthy: A timing-and-accuracy passer drafted on projection and delayed by injury — the 2025 debut was competence without flourish. The evaluation is genuinely just beginning.
reports.T.J. Hockenson: A complete tight end whose game was built on volume and reliability — the knee injury marks a clean before-and-after in the production. The route feel remains; the burst that created the separation is the question.
reports.J.K. Dobbins: Per-carry brilliance interrupted by the cruelest injury file at the position — every healthy stretch has produced at a lead-back level. The talent was never once the problem.
reports.Rhamondre Stevenson: A jump-cut power back with soft hands — the every-down skill set that keeps earning roles without ever commanding one. The profile is dependable committee-lead, and it has never pretended otherwise.
reports.Mark Andrews: A box-out target-winner whose game was built on trust with one quarterback in one scheme — both of which have moved on in stages. The decline is real; the red-zone instincts are the last durable asset.
reports.Michael Wilson: A crafty boundary receiver with strong hands and better route pacing than the athletic testing suggested — the steady growth is the story. A possession WR3 arc pointing quietly upward.
reports.Khalil Shakir: Buffalo's chain-mover — quick separation, sure hands, zero vertical dimension. The role is the most secure and most capped thing about him.
reports.Travis Kelce: The greatest route-running tight end ever, now operating on guile and Mahomes chemistry alone — and still converting. Every season is a curtain call with production.
reports.Zach Charbonnet: A power back with receiving polish who has out-produced his draft-day doubters every time the touches arrived — the Walker trade finally hands him the backfield. The profile always graded starter; now the depth chart agrees.
A big-slot possession target who does his best work in traffic — the alpha years in Indianapolis were built on volume and toughness, not separation. Pittsburgh gets the professional; the explosion was never the product.
reports.Tua Tagovailoa: An anticipation-and-accuracy passer whose entire value proposition is timing within a friendly scheme — Atlanta is the last test of whether the skill travels. The concussion file shadows every snap.
